title: Barcelona in 48 Hours
intro: Barcelona is a city built on arguments — between the sea and the hills, between the medieval grid and Cerdà's chamfered corners, between Gaudí and every straight line he ever met — and the perfect weekend picks a side in all of them. Day 1 runs the canon: a first-slot ticket into the Sagrada Família before the light gets crowded, the dragon roof of Passeig de Gràcia, a market counter lunch, and an evening that drifts from Gothic alleys to cava poured from the barrel in El Born. Day 2 belongs to the other Barcelona: Gaudí's hilltop park at opening, vermouth at noon in a Gràcia bodega the way the locals insist it be done, paella with sand in view, a cable car over the harbour to Montjuïc, and a last night of one-euro pintxos in Poble Sec. Two days, eleven stops, and one long argument between genius and gravity.

days.label: Day 1
days.subtitle: Gaudí's Masterpiece, the Old City & an El Born Evening

blocks.title: First Light Through the Stone Forest
blocks.body: Book the first timed slot of the day and be at the Nativity façade fifteen minutes early with a croissant in hand, because 9 a.m. is when the basilica belongs to you: low eastern sun pours through the blue-and-green glass of the Nativity side and sets the columned nave glowing like a forest at dawn. Gaudí worked on this building for 43 years knowing he would never finish it; 144 years after the first stone, the finish line is finally in sight — the central Torre de Jesucrist is being crowned this year, in the centenary of the architect's death, which will make this the tallest church on earth at 172.5 metres. Take the audio guide, take your time, and exit through the Passion façade to feel Subirachs' angular grief as the counterweight to all that morning joy.
blocks.tip: Tickets sell out days ahead in summer — book the 9 a.m. entry online the moment your dates are fixed. The tower add-on is worth it for the spiral descent alone; choose the Nativity tower for the older Gaudí-era views.

blocks.title: The Dragon Roof on the Block of Discord
blocks.body: Walk or ride two stops down to Passeig de Gràcia, the boulevard where Barcelona's industrial barons hired rival architects to out-dazzle each other, and commit to the weekend's one true splurge: Casa Batlló. The ticket price stings and the house is worth every cèntim — a marine dreamscape of bone-white balconies, a light well tiled in forty shades of blue, and a roof ridged like the dragon slain by Sant Jordi, Catalonia's patron saint. The immersive rooms added in recent years divide purists, but standing in the noble floor's whale-jaw window looking down on the boulevard is as close as architecture gets to being underwater. Afterwards, walk three blocks up to see Casa Milà (La Pedrera) heaving like a stone wave on its corner — the exterior costs nothing.
blocks.tip: Book the earliest 'Be the First' or standard slot you can and skip the queue entirely. If you'd rather spend the splurge on La Pedrera, its warrior-chimney rooftop is the better terrace — pick one and admire the other from the pavement.

blocks.title: Counter Lunch in the Belly of the City
blocks.placeName: Mercat de Sant Josep de la Boqueria
blocks.body: Stroll down to La Rambla and duck under the stained-glass arch of the Boqueria, the most famous market in Spain and still — despite the smoothie stalls out front — a working cathedral of food. Push past the first two aisles of tourist fruit cups into the interior, where the real market lives: pyramids of ñora peppers, counters of glistening gambas de Palamós, and jamón carvers working with surgical calm. Claim a stool at El Quim de la Boqueria and order what the kitchen is proudest of that day — the fried eggs with baby squid (ous amb xipirons) are the house legend, chased with a cold caña while the market roars around you. This is lunch as contact sport, and it is glorious.
blocks.tip: Counters here are first-come, hover-and-pounce — go before 1 p.m. or expect a wait. If the Saturday crush is too much, the wavy-roofed Mercat de Santa Caterina near the cathedral does the same magic at half the density.

blocks.title: Two Thousand Years in a Half-Mile of Alleys
blocks.body: Cross La Rambla and surrender to the Barri Gòtic, where Roman Barcino, medieval Barcelona and a good deal of romantic 1920s reconstruction share the same shadowed lanes. Skip the map: aim loosely for the cathedral and let the alleys do the routing. Find the four Roman columns of the Temple of Augustus hiding inside a courtyard on Carrer del Paradís, pass under the much-photographed neo-Gothic bridge on Carrer del Bisbe, and stop in Plaça de Sant Felip Neri, the quarter's most haunting square, its church walls still scarred by a 1938 bombing. Finish at the cathedral's geese-guarded cloister — thirteen white geese, one for each year of the martyred Santa Eulàlia's life — before the evening crowd arrives.
blocks.tip: The wander is free; the cathedral asks a modest entry that includes the rooftop lift, worth it for gargoyle-level views. Pickpockets work these lanes like professionals because they are — bags zipped, phones pocketed.

blocks.title: A Basilica at Golden Hour, Then Cava from the Barrel
blocks.body: Cross Via Laietana into El Born as the light turns honeyed and step first into Santa Maria del Mar, the fishermen's basilica raised in just 55 years by the people of the Ribera — stone hauled from Montjuïc on porters' backs — and the purest Catalan Gothic interior in existence: bare, soaring, and lit like the inside of a shell. Then walk one minute up Carrer de Montcada, past the medieval palaces that house the Picasso Museum, to the blue-tiled doorway of El Xampanyet, pouring its own fizzy house cava since 1929. There are no tables to speak of; you stand shoulder to shoulder with half the neighbourhood, drink from a saucer-shaped glass, and eat anchovies that justify their fame. It is loud, cramped, unbookable and perfect.
blocks.tip: The basilica is free to enter in the evening visiting window before it closes; do it first. El Xampanyet opens at 7 p.m. — arrive on the hour or embrace the scrum.

blocks.title: Dinner the Way El Born Does It
blocks.body: Barcelona eats late, so you're right on time. Two minutes from the Picasso Museum, Bar del Pla looks like a neighbourhood tapas bar and cooks like something far more ambitious: this is Catalan comfort food run through a chef's imagination, backed by one of the old city's best natural wine lists. Order the ous estrellats with foie, the crispy pig's trotters, the squid-ink croquettes that arrive jet black and molten, and whatever the blackboard is pushing. The room hums with a mix of locals and the happily lost, the vermut is house-made, and nobody will bring you a bill until you ask for it twice. End the night with a slow walk through the Born's lamplit lanes — the medieval market hall of El Born glows beautifully after dark.
blocks.tip: Book a few days ahead for 9 or 9:30 p.m., or eat at the bar if you arrive as a walk-in. Trust the server on wine — the list runs deep into small Catalan producers you won't see again.

days.label: Day 2
days.subtitle: Park Güell at Opening, Gràcia Vermut, the Sea & a Poble Sec Night

blocks.title: The City from Gaudí's Serpent Bench
blocks.body: Book the first timed entry to the Monumental Zone and take a taxi up the hill — the metro-plus-escalators route eats your golden hour. At opening, the famous mosaic salamander poses without a crowd, the Hypostyle Room's tilted columns hold up their ceiling of shattered-tile rosettes in silence, and the serpentine bench on the main terrace is yours: sit at its curve with the whole city rolled out below, from the Sagrada Família's cranes to the sea. The park began as a failed luxury housing estate — only two plots ever sold — which is why Gaudí's grand entrance pavilions, viaducts and colonnades now frame nothing but morning light and parakeets. An hour is enough; the magic is real but compact.
blocks.tip: First-slot tickets are timed and enforced — you get 30 minutes' grace, no more. Legs still fresh? The Bunkers del Carmel (Turó de la Rovira) viewpoint is a 20-minute climb east for the best free panorama in Barcelona.

blocks.title: Plaça-Hopping to the Vermut Hour
blocks.placeName: Vermut hour at Bodega Quimet in Gràcia
blocks.body: Walk downhill into Gràcia, the independent village Barcelona swallowed in 1897 and never quite digested, and give yourself an hour of aimless plaça-hopping: the clock tower of Plaça de la Vila de Gràcia, the terrace-ringed Plaça del Sol, the church-anchored calm of Plaça de la Virreina, with a flat white from SlowMov and a detour past the candy-striped façade of Casa Vicens, Gaudí's first house, along the way. Then honour the city's most civilised ritual. At Bodega Quimet, an old wine bodega with marble tables and barrels on the wall, order the house vermut over ice with an olive and a slice of orange, plus a gilda, some anchovies and a tin of good conserves. It is noon, the glass is cold and slightly bitter, and this — locals will tell you — is the entire point of Sunday.
blocks.tip: Vermut hour runs roughly noon to 2 p.m. and it is an aperitif, not lunch — snack lightly, because paella is coming. No table? Bar Calders in Sant Antoni pours an equally beloved vermut.

blocks.title: Paella with Your Feet Nearly in the Sand
blocks.body: Cab it down to the seafront, because Sunday paella by the Mediterranean is non-negotiable and the Escribà family — Barcelona pastry royalty since 1906 — runs the beachfront institution that does it properly. On the boardwalk at Bogatell beach, order the arròs a la barra or the classic seafood paella, made to order in wide steel pans and served with the socarrat (the caramelised crust at the bottom) intact, plus a bottle of cold Empordà white. Lunch will take two lazy hours; that is the design. Afterwards, walk it off south along the sand toward Barceloneta, past the volleyball nets and Rebecca Horn's rusted tower of stacked cubes, with the W Hotel's sail glinting at the far end of the beach.
blocks.tip: Book online a few days ahead for a front-row terrace table; rice arrives about 25 minutes after you order it. On a weekday, the counter at La Cova Fumada in Barceloneta — birthplace of the bomba — is the scrappy, brilliant alternative.

blocks.title: Over the Harbour to the Magic Mountain
blocks.body: From the Barceloneta end of the beach, ride the creaking red cabins of the Transbordador Aeri del Port — the 1929 harbour cable car — from its rocket-like tower straight over the masts of Port Vell to Miramar, where Montjuïc's gardens begin. Barcelona's hill of exhibitions and Olympics rewards wandering: drift through the cactus terraces and shaded gardens toward the white-walled Fundació Joan Miró if you have an hour for art, or head straight across the ridge to the grand staircase of the Museu Nacional d'Art de Catalunya (MNAC). Those palace steps, free to anyone, are the city's best sunset grandstand — the whole of Barcelona laid out down Avinguda de la Reina Maria Cristina, lights coming on from Tibidabo to the sea.
blocks.tip: The cable car queue moves slowly in high season — buy the one-way ticket online and go before 6 p.m. On show nights (Thursday to Saturday, extended in summer) the Magic Fountain below the MNAC steps erupts in its light-and-music spectacle, back in action after the drought years — check the schedule and time your descent.

blocks.title: The One-Euro Feast on Carrer de Blai
blocks.body: Walk ten minutes down Montjuïc's flank into Poble Sec and finish the weekend the way the neighbourhood does: grazing the pedestrian length of Carrer de Blai, where a dozen Basque-style pintxo bars pile their counters with toothpick-skewered inventions — tortilla wedges, goat's cheese with caramelised onion, txistorra sausage, things involving brie that have no name — at a euro or two apiece. The rules are simple: take a plate, help yourself, keep your toothpicks, and pay by the count at the end. Hop between Blai 9, La Tasqueta de Blai and whichever doorway smells best, with cañas or txakoli poured from a height. The street stays loud and happy past midnight, full of locals arguing about football and nobody in a hurry — the correct final scene for a Barcelona weekend.
blocks.tip: Peak crush is 9 to 11 p.m.; standing room is part of the fun. Still going at midnight? Sala Apolo, Poble Sec's storied ballroom-turned-club, is around the corner — Sunday's 'Nasty Mondays' warm-up crowd is a Barcelona institution.
